blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذَا يَغْشَىٰ (١)
By the night when it envelops, (1)
وَٱلنَّهَارِ إِذَا تَجَلَّىٰ (٢)
by the day when it brightens, (2)
وَمَا خَلَقَ ٱلذَّكَرَ وَٱلْأُنثَىٰٓ (٣)
by Him who created the male and the female: (3)
إِنَّ سَعْيَكُمْ لَشَتَّىٰ (٤)
your endeavours are indeed diverse. (4)
فَأَمَّا مَنْ أَعْطَىٰ وَٱتَّقَىٰ (٥)
As for him who gives and is Godwary (5)
وَصَدَّقَ بِٱلْحُسْنَىٰ (٦)
and confirms the best promise, (6)
فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُۥ لِلْيُسْرَىٰ (٧)
We will surely ease him toward facility. (7)
وَأَمَّا مَنۢ بَخِلَ وَٱسْتَغْنَىٰ (٨)
But as for him, who is stingy and self-complacent, (8)
وَكَذَّبَ بِٱلْحُسْنَىٰ (٩)
and denies the best promise, (9)
فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُۥ لِلْعُسْرَىٰ (١٠)
We will surely ease him toward hardship. (10)
وَمَا يُغْنِى عَنْهُ مَالُهُۥٓ إِذَا تَرَدَّىٰٓ (١١)
His wealth will not avail him when he perishes. (11)

Content and Virtue of Surah Al-Layl

This Surah, being one of the Meccan Surahs, possesses the characteristics of Meccan Surahs: short verse segments, intense content, and primarily focused on the Resurrection, divine reward and punishment, and their underlying factors and causes.

At the beginning, after mentioning three oaths, it divides people into two groups:

The God-fearing spenders, and the miserly who deny the reward of the Resurrection. It considers the end of the first group to be happiness, ease, and tranquility, while the end of the second group is deemed hardship, constriction, and misery.

In another part of this Surah, after pointing out that the guidance of His servants is upon Allah, He warns everyone of the blazing fire of Hell.

In the final section, He introduces those who will burn in this fire and the group that will be saved from it by mentioning their attributes.

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, it is narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said:

"من قرأها اعطاه اللَّه حتى يرضى، و عافاه من العسر و يسر له اليسر"

"Whoever recites this Surah, Allah will grant him so much that he becomes satisfied, protect him from hardships, and make the path of life easy for him." [1]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 499.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ

وَ اللَّيْلِ إِذا يَغْشى‌ (1) وَ النَّهارِ إِذا تَجَلَّى (2) وَ ما خَلَقَ الذَّكَرَ وَ الْأُنْثى‌ (3) إِنَّ سَعْيَكُمْ لَشَتَّى (4)

فَأَمَّا مَنْ أَعْطى‌ وَ اتَّقى‌ (5) وَ صَدَّقَ بِالْحُسْنى‌ (6) فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُ لِلْيُسْرى‌ (7) وَ أَمَّا مَنْ بَخِلَ وَ اسْتَغْنى‌ (8) وَ كَذَّبَ بِالْحُسْنى‌ (9)

فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُ لِلْعُسْرى‌ (10) وَ ما يُغْنِي عَنْهُ مالُهُ إِذا تردى (11)

Occasion of Revelation of Surah Al-Layl

Commentators have narrated an occasion of revelation for this entire Surah from Ibn Abbas, which we present according to what the late Tabarsi has recorded in Majma' al-Bayan:

"There was a man among the Muslims who had a branch of one of his date palms hanging over the house of a poor man with a large family. Whenever the owner of the palm climbed the tree to pick the dates, sometimes a few dates would fall into the poor man's house, and his children would pick them up. The man would climb down from the palm and take the dates from their hands—and he was so miserly and cold-hearted that if he saw a date in the mouth of one of them, he would put his finger inside the child's mouth to pull the date out!

The poor man complained to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: 'Go, and I will look into your matter.' Then He met the owner of the palm and said: 'Will you give me this tree whose branches hang over the house of so-and-so, in exchange for a palm tree in Paradise for you?' The man said: 'I have many palm trees, and the dates of none are as good as this tree (and I am not willing to make such a deal).'

One of the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله heard this and said: 'O Messenger of Allah! If I go and buy this tree from this man and hand it over, will You grant me the same thing You were offering him?' He replied: 'Yes.'

That man went and saw the owner of the palm and spoke with him. The owner of the palm said: 'Do you know that Muhammad offered me a palm tree in Paradise in exchange for this (and I did not accept), saying that I enjoy the dates of this one very much and although I have many palms, none have dates as good as this?'

The buyer asked: 'Do you want to sell it or not?' He said: 'I will not sell it unless you give me an amount I don't think anyone would give.' He asked: 'What amount?' He said: 'Forty palm trees.'

The buyer was surprised and said: 'What a heavy price you demand for a palm tree that is leaning—forty palms!'

Then, after a short silence, he said: 'Very well, I will give you forty palms.'

The (greedy) seller said: 'If you are telling the truth, call some people as witnesses!' It happened that a group was passing by; he called them and took them as witnesses to this transaction.

Then he came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: 'O Messenger of Allah! The palm tree has come into my possession, and I present it (to Your blessed presence).'

The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله went to the poor family and said to the head of the house: 'This palm tree is for you and your children.'

It was here that Surah Al-Layl was revealed (and said what needed to be said regarding the miserly and the generous)."

In some narrations, it is mentioned that the buyer was a person named "Abu al-Dahdah." [1]

Commentary

Piety and Divine Assistance

Again, at the beginning of this Surah, we encounter three thought-provoking oaths by the "creatures" and the "Creator of the universe." He says:

"By the night when it covers [the world]" (وَ اللَّيْلِ إِذا يَغْشى‌).

The expression "yaghsha" (covers) might be because the darkness of the night falls like a curtain over half of the globe and places it under its cover,

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 501.

or because the face of the day or the face of the world-illuminating sun is covered by its arrival. In any case, it is an allusion to the importance of the night and its effective role in human life—from moderating the sun's heat to the issue of tranquility and stillness for all living beings under its shadow, as well as the worship of the night-vigilant who are awake of heart and aware.

Then He moves to another oath and adds: "And by the day when it appears and becomes manifest" (وَ النَّهارِ إِذا تَجَلَّى‌) [1]. This is from the moment the dawn breaks the dark veil of the night, pushes back the darkness, and becomes dominant over the entire expanse of the sky, drowning everything in light and brightness—the same light that is the secret of movement and life and the source of nourishment for all living creatures. In the Holy Quran, much attention is drawn to the system of "light" and "darkness" and their impact on human life, for they are two great permanent blessings and two of the important signs of the Lord.

And then He moves to the final oath, saying: "And by Him Who

[^1]: It is noteworthy that "yaghsha" is mentioned as a "present/future" verb (mudari'), while "tajalla" is in the past tense. Some have said this is because at the time the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) mission began, the darkness of ignorance had covered everywhere; however, in this case, swearing by such darkness does not seem fitting. It is better to say that this past tense verb, because it follows the conditional "idha," conveys the meaning of the present/future, or that it was originally "tatajalla" with one "ta" omitted, though in that case, the verb would be feminine and its subject could not be "nahar" (day) but must be implied as "when the sun manifests within it."

created the male and the female" (وَ ما خَلَقَ الذَّكَرَ وَ الْأُنْثى‌).

For the existence of these two genders in the realms of "humans," "animals," and "plants," and the transformations that occur from the moment of conception until birth, and the characteristics each of the two genders possesses in accordance with their activities and programs, and the numerous secrets hidden in the concept of gender—all are signs and verses from the great world of creation, through which one can become aware of the greatness of its Creator.

The use of "ma" (that which/He who) here regarding Allah is an allusion to the extraordinary greatness of His Pure Essence and the ambiguity that prevails in this regard, such that it makes Him beyond imagination, comparison, conjecture, and fancy.

Some have also said "ma" here is infinitive (masdariyah), and thus the meaning of the sentence would be: "By the creation of the male and the female," but this possibility seems weak.

In reality, the first and second oaths refer to the "horizonal signs" (ayat afaqi) and the third oath refers to the "internal signs" (ayat anfusi).

Finally, He reaches the ultimate goal of these oaths and says: "Indeed, your efforts and struggles in life are diverse and various" (إِنَّ سَعْيَكُمْ لَشَتَّى‌). The direction of efforts and their results are also completely different and varied. This is an indication that you will not remain still in life anyway; you will certainly engage in effort and struggle, and the God-given powers that are the capital of your existence will be spent in some path. See in which path your effort and struggle lie, toward which direction, and with what result? Lest you sell all your capital and talents for a small price or waste them in vain.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 27, p. 73.

"Shatta" is the plural of "shatit" from the root "shatt" (rhyming with shatt), meaning to scatter a crowd, and here it refers to the difference in people's efforts in terms of quality, goal-setting, and their results.

Then He divides people into two groups and enumerates the characteristics of each, saying: "As for him who gives [in the way of Allah] and guards against evil [practicing piety]..." (فَأَمَّا مَنْ أَعْطى‌ وَ اتَّقى‌).

"And believes in the best [divine reward]..." (وَ صَدَّقَ بِالْحُسْنى‌).

"We will make smooth for him the path of ease" and guide him toward the eternal Paradise (فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُ لِلْيُسْرى‌). The meaning of "a'ta" (gave) is spending in the way of Allah and helping the needy.

The emphasis on "taqwa" (piety) following it may refer to the necessity of a pure intention and sincere motive when spending, acquiring wealth through legitimate means, spending it also in a legitimate way, and being free from any sense of obligation-laying (mann) or annoyance, for all these attributes are gathered under the title of piety.

Some have also said: "a'ta" refers to financial worships, and "attaqa" refers to other worships, performing obligations, and abandoning prohibitions; however, the first interpretation is more consistent with both the appearance of the verse and the occasion of revelation mentioned above.

Confirming the "husna" ("husna" is the feminine of "ahsan," meaning the best)

refers to faith in the good divine rewards, just as it was mentioned in the occasion of revelation that "Abu al-Dahdah" spent his wealth with faith in Allah's rewards. In verse 95 of Surah An-Nisa, we also read: وَ كُلًّا وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الْحُسْنى‌: "To each, Allah has promised the best reward" (husna in this verse also means the good reward).

Some have also said: it means the "best Sharia," i.e., faith in the "religion of Islam," which is the best of religions.

And some have interpreted it as the word "La ilaha illa Allah" or the two testimonies (Shahadatayn).

However, considering the context of the verses and the occasion of revelation, and the mention of "husna" meaning good reward in many verses of the Quran, the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

The phrase "فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُ لِلْيُسْرى‌" may refer to divine success (tawfiq) and making the matter of obedience easy for such individuals, or opening the way to Paradise for them and the angels welcoming them with greetings and peace, or all of these!

It is certain that those who take the path of spending and piety, and are faithful and encouraged by the great divine rewards, difficulties become easy for them, and they enjoy a special tranquility in this world and the Hereafter.

Beyond all this, it is possible that financial spending may initially be heavy and difficult for human nature, but with repetition and continuing the path, it becomes so easy for him that he enjoys it.

How many generous individuals are there who are happy at the presence of a guest at their table and, conversely, are upset if a guest does not arrive for them one day; this too is a kind of easing of difficulties for them.

One should also not neglect the point that, fundamentally, faith in the Resurrection and the great divine rewards

makes enduring various difficulties easy for man; he places not only his "wealth" but also his "life" on the tray of sincerity and participates in the field of Jihad out of love for martyrdom, enjoying his own self-sacrifice.

"Yusra" is from the root "yusr," which originally means saddling a horse and bridling it and preparing it for riding, and subsequently, it has been applied to any easy and simple task. [1]

In the following verses, He addresses the opposite of this group, saying: "But as for him who is miserly and considers himself self-sufficient [through this means]..." (وَ أَمَّا مَنْ بَخِلَ وَ اسْتَغْنى‌).

"And denies the best reward..." (وَ كَذَّبَ بِالْحُسْنى‌).

"We will soon make smooth for him the path of hardship" (فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُ لِلْعُسْرى‌). "Bukhl" (miserliness) here is the opposite of "a'ta" (giving) which was stated in the first group (the group of the happy generous ones). "And istaghna" (seeks self-sufficiency) is either an excuse for being miserly and a means for amassing wealth, or it refers to him considering himself independent of divine rewards—unlike the first group whose eyes are constantly on Allah's grace—or he sees himself as self-sufficient from obeying the Lord and is constantly tainted by sin.

Among these three interpretations, the first seems more appropriate, although a combination of all three is also possible.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Kashshaf, Vol. 4, p. 762.

The meaning of denying "husna" is the denial of the rewards of the Resurrection, or the denial of the religion and the good way of the prophets.

The expression "فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُ لِلْعُسْرى‌," which actually contains two seemingly contradictory terms (We make their path toward difficulties easy), is the opposite of "فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُ لِلْيُسْرى‌." This is in the sense that Allah includes the first group in His successes (tawfiqat) and makes traversing the path of obedience and spending easy for them so they may be freed from difficulties in life; but the second group has their successes withdrawn, traversing the path becomes difficult for them, and they will face hardships in this world and the Hereafter. Fundamentally, for these faithless misers, performing good deeds and especially spending in the way of Allah is a hard and difficult task, whereas for the first group, it is exhilarating and soul-refreshing. [1]

And at the end of the verse under discussion, He warns these blind-hearted misers, saying: "And his wealth will not avail him when he falls [into the grave or Hell]" (وَ ما يُغْنِي عَنْهُ مالُهُ إِذا تَرَدَّى‌).

He can neither take this wealth with him from this world, nor, if he could, would it prevent his fall into the fire of Hell.

"Ma" at the beginning of this verse may be "negative" (as we said above)

[^1]: "Yusra" and "Usra" are both feminine forms (the masculine forms are "Aysar" and "A'sar"). Their mention in the feminine form is either because the described object is "the collection of deeds" (al-a'mal), and the implication would be "We will make the easy deeds easy for him" or "the difficult deeds," or the collection of life's issues and events. If the described object is singular, it might be "tariqah" (way) or "khullah" (trait) and the like.

or for "interrogative" denial, meaning: what benefit will his wealth be to him when falling into the grave or Hell?

"Taradda" is from the root "rada'at" and "rada," meaning destruction, and it also means falling from a height that causes destruction; rather, some consider its origin to be "falling," and since falling from a high point causes destruction, it has also come to mean destruction. In the verse under discussion, it may mean falling into the grave, or Hell, or destruction in the sense of punishment.

Thus, the Quran in these verses speaks of two groups: a group that is faithful, pious, and generous, and a group that is faithless, impious, and miserly—the examples of both groups are clearly reflected in the occasion of revelation.

The first group, by utilizing divine successes, traverses their path with ease and moves toward Paradise and its blessings, while the second group faces a multitude of difficulties in life, amasses vast wealth and leaves it behind, and gains nothing but regret, sorrow, burden, and divine punishment, and purchases no lasting goods.

Ayahs

إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا لَلْهُدَىٰ (١٢)
Indeed guidance rests with Us, (12)
وَإِنَّ لَنَا لَلْـَٔاخِرَةَ وَٱلْأُولَىٰ (١٣)
and to Us belong the world and the Hereafter. (13)
فَأَنذَرْتُكُمْ نَارًا تَلَظَّىٰ (١٤)
So I warn you of a blazing fire, (14)
لَا يَصْلَىٰهَآ إِلَّا ٱلْأَشْقَى (١٥)
which none shall enter except the most wretched [of persons] (15)
ٱلَّذِى كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّىٰ (١٦)
—he who impugns [God’s prophets] and turns his back. (16)
وَسَيُجَنَّبُهَا ٱلْأَتْقَى (١٧)
The Godwary [person] will be spared of that (17)
ٱلَّذِى يُؤْتِى مَالَهُۥ يَتَزَكَّىٰ (١٨)
—he who gives his wealth to purify himself (18)
وَمَا لِأَحَدٍ عِندَهُۥ مِن نِّعْمَةٍ تُجْزَىٰٓ (١٩)
and does not expect any reward from anyone, (19)
إِلَّا ٱبْتِغَآءَ وَجْهِ رَبِّهِ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ (٢٠)
but seek only the pleasure of their Lord, the Most Exalted, (20)
وَلَسَوْفَ يَرْضَىٰ (٢١)
and, surely, soon they will be well-pleased. (21)

Commentary

Spending and Avoiding the Fire of Hell

Following the previous verses which divided people into two groups—the generous believer and the faithless miser—and described the fate of each, the verses under discussion first address the point that Our task is guidance, not compulsion or coercion. It is your duty to decide and be a traveler of the path; furthermore, traversing this path is for your own benefit, and We have no need for it.

He says: "Indeed, [it is] for Us to [give] guidance" (إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا لَلْهُدَىٰ).

Whether it be guidance through creation (primordial nature and intellect) or through legislation (the Book and the Sunnah), We have said what was necessary in this regard and have fulfilled its right.

"And indeed, to Us belongs the Hereafter and the first [life]" (وَإِنَّ لَنَا لَلْآخِرَةَ وَالْأُولَىٰ) [^1]. We have no need for your faith or obedience; neither does your obedience bring Us benefit, nor does your disobedience cause Us harm. All these programs are for your benefit and for your own sake.

According to this interpretation, guidance here means "showing the way." It is also possible that the aim of these two verses is to encourage generous believers and emphasize that We will grant them further guidance and make the path easy for them in this world and the next; and since the world and the hereafter belong to Us, We are capable of doing so.

It is true that the world precedes the hereafter in terms of time, but in terms of importance and the ultimate goal, the primary objective is the hereafter, and for this reason, it has been mentioned first.

And since one of the branches of guidance is warning and admonition, He adds in the next verse: "So I have warned you of a Fire which is blazing"! (فَأَنْذَرْتُكُمْ نَاراً تَلَظَّىٰ). "Talazza" is derived from the root "laza" (rhyming with qada), meaning a pure flame. We know that pure flames, free from any smoke, possess greater heat and intensity. Sometimes the word "Laza" is also applied to Hell itself [^2].

[^1]: The "Lam" in "lal-akhirah" and also in "lal-huda" (previous verse) is apparently the "Lam of emphasis" which here precedes the "Ism of Inna," even though it usually precedes the "Khabar." This is because, as stated in some books of grammar, whenever the Khabar of Inna is placed first, the Lam precedes the Ism.

[^2]: "Talazza" was originally "Tatalazza," where one of the two "Ta"s was dropped for ease of pronunciation.

Then, referring to the group that enters this ignited and burning fire, He says: "None will [enter to] burn therein except the most wretched" (لَا يَصْلَاهَا إِلَّا الْأَشْقَى).

In describing the most wretched (ashqa), He says: "Who had denied and turned away" (الَّذِي كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّىٰ). Therefore, the criterion for happiness and wretchedness is disbelief and faith, along with the practical consequences these two entail. Truly, one who ignores all those signs of guidance and opportunities for faith and piety is the clear manifestation of the "most wretched" and the unhappiest of people.

In the phrase "who had denied and turned away," "denial" (takdhib) may refer to "disbelief" (kufr), and "turning away" (tawalli) may refer to abandoning righteous deeds, as this is the necessary consequence of disbelief.

It is also possible that both refer to the abandonment of faith, in such a way that first he denies the Prophet of Allah, and then turns his back and distances himself from him forever.

Many commentators have raised an objection here and addressed it: that the above verses indicate the Fire of Hell is exclusive to the disbelievers, which contradicts what is understood from other Quranic verses and the collection of Islamic narrations—that sinful believers also have a share of the Fire of Hell. Consequently, some deviant groups who believe that no sin causes harm as long as faith exists (known as the "Murji'ah") have used these verses to argue for their position.

In response, two points must be noted: First, the meaning of "entering the fire" here is "abiding eternally" (khulud), and we know that eternal abiding is exclusive to the disbelievers. The evidence for this is the verses showing that non-disbelievers also enter Hell.

Second, the above verses and the subsequent verses which state that being kept away from the Fire is exclusive to the "most pious" (atqa) mean that, overall, the text only intends to describe the state of two groups: the faithless miserly group, and the group of generous and highly pious believers. Of these two groups, only the first enters Hell and the second enters Paradise. Thus, the third group—sinful believers—is not mentioned at all.

In other words, the "restriction" (hasr) here is a "relative restriction" (hasr idafi), as if Paradise was created only for the second group and Hell only for the first. With this explanation, the answer to another objection regarding the "contradiction" between these verses and future verses that restrict salvation to the "most pious" also becomes clear.

Then He speaks of the group that is kept away from this blazing, burning fire, saying: "But the most righteous will be kept away from it" (وَسَيُجَنَّبُهَا الْأَتْقَى).

"[He] who gives [from] his wealth to purify himself" (الَّذِي يُؤْتِي مَالَهُ يَتَزَكَّىٰ). The expression "yatazakka" (to purify himself) is actually an allusion to the intention of seeking proximity to Allah (qasdu al-qurbah) and pure sincerity, whether this phrase means seeking spiritual and soul growth or obtaining the purity of wealth. This is because "tazkiyah" means both "to make grow" and "to purify." In Surah At-Tawbah, verse 103, we also read: "Take, [O Muhammad], from their wealth a charity by which you purify them and cause them increase, and invoke [Allah's blessings] upon them. Indeed, your invocations are reassurance for them."

Then, to emphasize the issue of their sincerity of intention in the spending they do, He adds: "And not [giving] for anyone who has [done him] a favor to be rewarded" (وَمَا لِأَحَدٍ عِنْدَهُ مِنْ نِعْمَةٍ تُجْزَىٰ).

"But only seeking the countenance of his Lord, the Most High" (إِلَّا ابْتِغَاءَ وَجْهِ رَبِّهِ الْأَعْلَىٰ). In other words: many instances of spending among people are a response to a similar act of spending previously done by the other party. Of course, gratitude and responding to kindness with kindness is a good deed, but it is separate from the sincere spending of the pious. The above verses say: the spending of the pious believers on others is neither out of ostentation nor to repay past services; rather, its motive is solely and exclusively to seek the pleasure of Allah, and it is this that gives such spending extraordinary value.

The expression "Wajh" (Countenance) here means "Essence" (Dhat), and the intention is the pleasure and satisfaction of His Holy Essence.

The expression "his Lord, the Most High" (Rabbihi al-A'la) shows that this spending is performed with complete gnosis (ma'rifah), while being aware of both the Lordship of the Sustainer and His supreme station.

Furthermore, this exception negates all kinds of deviant intentions, such as spending for a good reputation, attracting people's attention, or gaining social status, because its meaning restricts the motive of their spending to seeking the pleasure of the Lord [^1].

Finally, in the last verse of this Surah, He mentions the great and peerless reward of this group and says in a short sentence: "And he is going to be satisfied" (وَلَسَوْفَ يَرْضَىٰ). Yes, just as he worked for the pleasure of Allah, Allah will also make him satisfied—an absolute and unconditional satisfaction, a vast and unlimited satisfaction, a meaningful satisfaction in which all blessings are gathered, a satisfaction that is impossible for us to even imagine today. And what blessing can be imagined greater or higher than this?

Indeed, the Lord is the Most High, and His reward and recompense are also the most high, and nothing higher than the absolute satisfaction and pleasure of the servants can be imagined.

Some commentators have also suggested that the pronoun in "yarda" (he will be satisfied) refers to Allah, meaning that soon Allah will be satisfied with this group, which is also a great and peerless gift—that Allah the Great and the Lord Most High be satisfied and pleased with such a servant, and that too with an absolute and unconditional satisfaction. Certainly, following this Divine satisfaction is the satisfaction and pleasure of that pious believing servant, as these two are inseparable, just as we read in Surah Al-Bayyinah, verse 8: "Allah being pleased with them and they with Him," or in Surah Al-Fajr, verse 28: "well-pleased and well-pleasing." However, the first interpretation is more appropriate.

Key Points

A Discussion Regarding the Occasion of Revelation of Surah Al-Layl

Fakhr al-Razi says: "Sunni commentators generally believe that the meaning of 'the most pious' (atqa) in 'But the most righteous will be kept away from it' is Abu Bakr, and the Shi'a generally deny this tradition and say it was revealed concerning Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام" [^1]. Then, in an analysis peculiar to himself, he says: "The Islamic Ummah (both Sunni and Shi'a) agree that the best of people after the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله was either Abu Bakr or Ali. This verse cannot be applied to Ali, because the Quran says regarding this 'most pious' individual: 'And not [giving] for anyone who has [done him] a favor to be rewarded.' This attribute does not apply to Ali عليه السلام, because the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had the right of favor over him! But the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله not only had no right of material favor over Abu Bakr, but on the contrary, he [Abu Bakr] used to spend on the Messenger of Allah and had the right of favor! The result is that the manifestation of 'the most pious' is Abu Bakr, and since 'atqa' means the most pious of people, his superiority is proven!" [^2]. Although we do not wish to enter into such issues extensively in the discussions of this Tafsir, the insistence of some commentators on proving their preconceptions through Quranic verses—to the extent that they even use expressions that are not appropriate for the lofty station of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—prompts us to mention a few points here.

Firstly, Fakhr al-Razi's claim that there is a "Sunni" consensus that this verse was revealed about Abu Bakr contradicts what some famous Sunni commentators have explicitly stated. For instance, "Qurtubi" in his Tafsir narrates a tradition from "Ibn Abbas" that this entire Surah (Surah Al-Layl) was revealed concerning "Abu al-Dahdah" (whose story we mentioned at the beginning of the Surah) [^1]. Specifically, when he reaches the verse "But the most righteous will be kept away from it," he again says the intended person is "Abu al-Dahdah," although he narrates from most commentators that it was revealed about Abu Bakr, but he himself did not accept this view.

Secondly, his claim that there is a Shi'a consensus that this verse was revealed about Ali عليه السلام is not correct, as many Shi'a commentators have mentioned and accepted the story of Abu al-Dahdah. Yes, in some narrations from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is mentioned that the "most pious" refers to his followers and Shi'a, and the one "who gives his wealth to purify himself" is the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام. However, it appears that these are not regarding the occasion of revelation but are rather applications to a clear and prominent manifestation.

Thirdly, without doubt, "atqa" in the above verse does not mean "the most pious of people" but rather its meaning is simply being "pious." A clear proof of this is that "ashqa" (the most wretched) in contrast to it does not mean "the worst of people," but rather refers to the disbelievers who were miserly in spending. Furthermore, this verse was revealed while the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was alive. Can Abu Bakr be placed even before the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله? Why should we, to prove our preconceptions and mentalities, use an expression that even harms the lofty station of the Prophet?

And if it is said that the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله account is separate, we ask: then why was the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله account not separated in the verse "And not [giving] for anyone who has [done him] a favor to be rewarded"? And why should we say that because Ali عليه السلام was a recipient of the Prophet's material favors, he cannot be included in the verse, just to exclude him?

Fourthly, who is there in his life who has not been the object of someone's kindness, and for whom no one has ever given a gift or hosted a feast? Was it really the case that Abu Bakr, in his entire life, never went to anyone's feast, never accepted a gift from anyone, nor any other material service? Is this believable? The conclusion is that the meaning of the verse "And not [giving] for anyone who has [done him] a favor to be rewarded" is not that no one has a right of favor over them, but rather that their spending is not because of a right of favor.

That is, if they spend on someone, it is only for Allah, not because of a service they wish to reward.

Fifthly, the verses of this Surah show that it was revealed regarding a two-polar event: one at the pole of "atqa" (the pious) and the other at the pole of "ashqa" (the wretched). If we consider the occasion of revelation to be the story of "Abu al-Dahdah," the issue is resolved. But if we say the intended person was Abu Bakr, the problem of "ashqa" remains: who is intended by it?!

The Shi'a do not insist that this specific verse is about Ali عليه السلام; the verses regarding his status are very numerous. However, if it were applied to Ali عليه السلام, the problem of "ashqa" would be resolved, because under verse 12 of Surah Ash-Shams ("When the most wretched of them was sent forth"), many narrations have been narrated through Sunni channels that the "most wretched" (ashqa) refers to the killer of Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام (these narrations, as we said, were collected by Hakim Haskani in Shawahid al-Tanzil).

In short, Fakhr al-Razi's statement and his analysis regarding this verse are very weak and contain numerous errors. Therefore, even some famous Sunni commentators like Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani did not favor this analysis and criticized it, saying: "The Imam [Fakhr al-Razi] argued from this that he [Abu Bakr] is the best of the Ummah, and mentioned that in the verses there is that which rejects the Shi'a view that it is about Ali, and he spoke at length on this and brought forth that which is not free from 'qil and qal' (dispute and objection)" [^1]. It should not be forgotten that Alusi himself is relatively prejudiced, yet he did not favor Fakhr al-Razi's analysis of this verse.

2- The Virtue of Spending in the Way of Allah

Spending and giving in the way of Allah and providing financial assistance to the deprived—especially those who maintain their dignity—coupled with sincerity of intention, are matters repeatedly emphasized in the verses of the Holy Quran and mentioned as signs of faith.

Islamic narrations are also full of emphasis in this regard, to the extent that they show in Islamic culture, financial spending—provided it has no motive other than the pleasure of the Lord and is free from any ostentation, reminder of favor, or injury—is among the best of deeds.

We complete this discussion by mentioning a few meaningful traditions:

1- In a narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "Indeed, the most beloved of deeds to Allah is bringing joy to a believer: satisfying the hunger of a Muslim or paying off his debt" [^1].

2- In a tradition from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated: "From faith is good character, feeding [the hungry], and the shedding of blood (sacrificing in the way of Allah)" [^2].

3- In another tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is narrated that he said: "I do not see anything equal to visiting a believer except feeding him, and it is a right upon Allah to feed whoever feeds a believer from the food of Paradise" [^3].

4- In another tradition from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is narrated that a man took the reins of the Prophet's mount and said: "O Messenger of Allah! Which deed is the best?" He replied: "Feeding [the hungry] and pleasantness of speech" [^4].

5- And finally, in a tradition from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Whoever provides for a household of Muslims for their day and night, Allah forgives his sins" [^1].

O Allah! Grant us all the success to step into this great good. O Lord! Increase the sincerity of our intentions in all our deeds. O God! We ask You to encompass us with Your blessing and mercy such that both You are pleased with us and we become pleased and satisfied.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Al-Layl.

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 74, Hadith 35, p. 365. [^2]: Ibid., Hadith 38. [^3]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on Feeding the Believer, Hadith 17. [^4]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 74, p. 388, Hadith 113. [^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 74, p. 389, Hadith 2.
